subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: This guide is written for the teacher or parent who will lead a young child (age 5–6) through Unit 5. Year 1 learners are usually pre-readers, so this unit is taught with you, not read alone by the child. Your role is to talk, show, and play alongside the child using the simple scripts and activities in this guide. The child's own work happens in the companion Activity Sheet and Practice documents.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 1 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Combine two groups of objects and count how many there are altogether.
Understand that addition means putting groups together.
Write an addition sentence using the + and = signs (for example, 3 + 2 = 5).
Add 1 more to any number from 1 to 9 and know it makes the next counting number.
Use a ten-frame and counters to add small numbers.
Understand that subtraction (take away) means removing some to find how many are left.
Write a subtraction sentence using the − and = signs (for example, 5 − 2 = 3).
Recognise number bonds and part-whole relationships for numbers to 10.
Begin to find all the ways to make a number, such as the bonds that make 10.

concepts.title: Addition as Combining
concepts.explanation: The first big idea is that adding means putting groups together to find how many there are altogether. A child who can count is ready for this: they simply count one group, count the other, push them together, and count them all. How to teach it: Put 3 dates in one small group and 2 dates in another. Count each group aloud with the child. Then say, ‘Let us combine them – push them together – and count how many altogether.’ Count all 5 together. Say clearly, ‘3 add 2 equals 5.’ Only after the child has done this…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “Here are 3 dates, and here are 2 more dates. Let us put them together.” You and child: push the groups together and count “one, two, three, four, five.” You: “So 3 add 2 equals… how many…

concepts.title: Adding 1 More
concepts.explanation: A special, powerful case of adding is adding 1 more. When you add 1 to any number, you always land on the next counting number. This links adding straight back to the counting the child already knows well, so it is a confidence-builder. How to teach it: Put 5 counters on the ten-frame. Ask the child to add 1 more and count: ‘6!’ Say, ‘5 add 1 equals 6 – the next counting number.’ Try it from 6, 7, 8 and 9. Compare it to climbing stairs: each ‘add 1’ is one step up. The child will soon predict the answer before…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “Put 7 stones in a row. Now add just 1 more stone.” You: “Count them. How many now?” Child: “Eight!” You: “7 add 1 equals 8. Adding 1 always gives the next number when we count.”

concepts.title: The Ten-Frame for Adding
concepts.explanation: A ten-frame (a 2×5 grid) helps the child ‘see’ an addition instead of counting from one every time. Fill the top row first, left to right, then the bottom row. How to teach it: To add 3 + 2, place 3 counters on the frame, then add 2 more. The top row fills to 5, so the child can see at a glance that 3 add 2 makes 5. For sums such as 6 + 4, filling the whole frame shows the answer is a full ten. The frame turns adding into a picture, which is far easier for a young mind to hold than abstract digits.
concepts.example: Always fill the frame the same way – top row left to right, then the bottom row – so the child pictures each number in the same steady shape every time. After a while, cover the frame for a moment and ask, ‘How many…

concepts.title: Part-Whole and Number Bonds
concepts.explanation: A number bond shows how a number is made from two parts that fit inside a whole. For example, 5 is made from 2 and 3, or from 1 and 4. Understanding that numbers are built from smaller parts is one of the most important ideas in early maths – it underpins adding, subtracting, and later mental strategies. How to teach it: Draw a simple part-whole mat – one big circle (the whole) joined to two small circles (the parts). Put 5 stones in the whole, then split them between the two parts: maybe 2 in one and 3 in the…
concepts.example: The number bonds for 10 deserve special attention, because they become so useful later. With the child, use counters to find every pair that makes 10: 1 and 9, 2 and 8, 3 and 7, 4 and 6, 5 and 5, and their partners the…

concepts.title: Subtraction as Take Away
concepts.explanation: The other half of this unit is subtraction, which for young children means take away: you start with a group, remove some, and find how many are left. Subtract is just another way of saying take away. As with adding, always start with real objects the child can physically remove. How to teach it: Put 7 stones on the mat. Ask the child to take away 2 and count how many are left. Say, ‘7 take away 2 equals 5.’ Then show the written sentence 7 − 2 = 5, pointing to each part. Use the ‘first, then, now’ story pattern…
concepts.example: The part-whole mat helps here too, worked the other way round. Put the whole amount in the big circle, move the part you are taking away into one part circle, and whatever is left slides into the other part circle. This…

concepts.title: There is a lovely investigation in the
concepts.explanation: There is a lovely investigation in the source book: Sumi says that when you add 1 more you make the next counting number. Ask the child, ‘Is Sumi correct?’ and let them test it with their own counters. Discovering that a rule is always true is deeply satisfying for a young child, and it plants the early idea that maths has dependable patterns you can rely on.
concepts.example: There is a lovely investigation in the source book: Sumi says that when you add 1 more you make the next counting number. Ask the child, ‘Is Sumi correct?’ and let them test it with their own counters. Discovering that a rule is always true is deeply satisfying for a young child, and it plants the early idea that maths has dependable patterns you can rely on.

activities.title: Practise together
activities.materials: Paper, pencil and familiar household objects
steps: Choose one idea from this unit.
Build or draw a model of it.
Explain your model to a family member.
Write one question of your own and solve it.

rules.title: There is a lovely investigation in the
rules.text: There is a lovely investigation in the source book: Sumi says that when you add 1 more you make the next counting number. Ask the child, ‘Is Sumi correct?’ and let them test it with their own counters. Discovering that a rule is always true is deeply satisfying for a young child, and it plants the early idea that maths has dependable patterns you can rely on.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Part-Whole and Number Bonds.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A number bond shows how a number is made from two parts that fit inside a whole. For example, 5 is made from 2 and 3, or from 1 and 4. Understanding that numbers are built from smaller parts is one of the most important ideas in early maths – it underpins adding, subtracting, and later mental strategies. How to teach it: Draw a simple part-whole mat – one big circle (the whole) joined to two small circles (the parts). Put 5 stones in the whole, then split them between the two parts: maybe 2 in one and 3 in the…

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Subtraction as Take Away.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The other half of this unit is subtraction, which for young children means take away: you start with a group, remove some, and find how many are left. Subtract is just another way of saying take away. As with adding, always start with real objects the child can physically remove. How to teach it: Put 7 stones on the mat. Ask the child to take away 2 and count how many are left. Say, ‘7 take away 2 equals 5.’ Then show the written sentence 7 − 2 = 5, pointing to each part. Use the ‘first, then, now’ story pattern…

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in There is a lovely investigation in the.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: There is a lovely investigation in the source book: Sumi says that when you add 1 more you make the next counting number. Ask the child, ‘Is Sumi correct?’ and let them test it with their own counters. Discovering that a rule is always true is deeply satisfying for a young child, and it plants the early idea that maths has dependable patterns you can rely on.

questions.question: In the number 54, what is the value of the digit 5?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: 5 is in the tens place, so it is worth 50.

questions.question: What does “Add / plus” mean?
options: How many when the groups are combined
Means the same as, or makes
Maths written with numbers and signs
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Add / plus means Put two groups together.

questions.question: What does “Altogether / total” mean?
options: How many when the groups are combined
Means the same as, or makes
Maths written with numbers and signs
questions.answer: How many when the groups are combined
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Altogether / total means How many when the groups are combined.

How many remain after taking away

selfAssessment: I can combine two groups of objects and count how many there are altogether.
I can understand that addition means putting groups together.
I can write an addition sentence using the + and = signs (for example, 3 + 2 = 5).
I can add 1 more to any number from 1 to 9 and know it makes the next counting number.
I can use a ten-frame and counters to add small numbers.
I can understand that subtraction (take away) means removing some to find how many are left.
I can write a subtraction sentence using the − and = signs (for example, 5 − 2 = 3).
I can recognise number bonds and part-whole relationships for numbers to 10.
